name: onesixtyone
title: onesixtyone
summary: onesixtyone - fast and simple SNMP scanner

commands.name: onesixtyone

arguments.description: debug mode, use twice for more information
arguments.raw: -d  debug mode, use twice for more information

arguments.description: specify an alternate destination SNMP port
arguments.raw: -p  specify an alternate destination SNMP port

arguments.description: quiet mode, do not print log to stdout, use with -o
arguments.raw: -q  quiet mode, do not print log to stdout, use with -o

arguments.description: short mode, only print IP addresses
arguments.raw: -s  short mode, only print IP addresses

commands.raw_help:
root@kali:~# onesixtyone -h
 onesixtyone 0.3.3 [options] <host> <community>
 -c <communityfile> file with community names to try
 -i <inputfile>     file with target hosts
 -o <outputfile>    output log
 -p                 specify an alternate destination SNMP port
 -d                 debug mode, use twice for more information
 
 -s                 short mode, only print IP addresses
 
 -w n               wait n milliseconds (1/1000 of a second) between sending packets (default 10)
 -q                 quiet mode, do not print log to stdout, use with -o
 host is either an IPv4 address or an IPv4 address and a netmask
 default community names are: public private
 
 Max number of hosts : 		65536
 Max community length: 		32
 Max number of communities: 	16384
 
 
 examples: onesixtyone 192.168.4.0/24 public
         onesixtyone -c dict.txt -i hosts -o my.log -w 100

local_help.man:
ONESIXTYONE(1)              General Commands Manual              ONESIXTYONE(1)

NAME
     onesixtyone - fast and simple SNMP scanner

SYNOPSIS
     onesixtyone [options] <host> <community>

OPTIONS
     -c <communityfile> file with community names to try

     -i <inputfile>
            file with target hosts

     -o <outputfile>
            output log

     -p     specify an alternate destination SNMP port

     -d     debug mode, use twice for more information

     -s     short mode, only print IP addresses

     -w n   wait  n  milliseconds  (1/1000 of a second) between sending packets
            (default 10)

     -q     quiet mode, do not print log to stdout, use with -o

     host is either an IPv4 address or an IPv4 address and  a  netmask  default
     community names are: public private

            Max number of hosts :           65535

            Max community length:           32

            Max number of communities:      16384

EXAMPLES
     onesixtyone 192.168.4.0/24 public

     onesixtyone -c dict.txt -i hosts -o my.log -w 100

COPYRIGHT
     License   GPLv2+:   GNU   GPL   version  2  or  later  <http://gnu.org/li-
     censes/gpl.html>

User Commands                      April 2019                    ONESIXTYONE(1)

binary_metadata.file_type: ELF 64-bit LSB pie executable, x86-64, version 1 (SYSV), dynamically linked, interpreter /lib64/ld-linux-x86-64.so.2, BuildID[sha1]=41508d0246620fcd4b5d4aafa94fbe9adb59121a, for GNU/Linux 3.2.0, stripped

apt_metadata.description:
fast and simple SNMP scanner
onesixtyone is a simple SNMP scanner which sends SNMP requests for the
sysDescr value asynchronously with user-adjustable sending times and
then logs the responses which gives the description of the software
running on the device.
.
Running onesixtyone on a class B network (switched 100Mbs with 1Gbs
backbone) with -w 10 gives a performance of 3 seconds per class C, with
no dropped packets, and all 65536 IP addresses were scanned in less than
13 minutes.

use_cases: When you need Kali Linux, use onesixtyone because onesixtyone - fast and simple SNMP scanner.
When you need a concrete invocation, run `onesixtyone [options] <host> <community>` as documented upstream for onesixtyone.
When you need a concrete invocation, run `onesixtyone $KFIOSA_TARGET_HOST/24 public` as documented upstream for onesixtyone.
When you need a concrete invocation, run `onesixtyone -c dict.txt -i hosts -o my.log -w 100` as documented upstream for onesixtyone.
When chaining Kali Linux tools, pair onesixtyone with sibling catalog entries sharing its attack_surface/phase_hint.
When validating lab setup, run `onesixtyone --help` (or the README entrypoint) and confirm required hardware/deps from the catalog.
